title: Reading and Use of English – Part 1
description: Read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap.

content.text: The story of tea (0) dates back nearly five thousand years to ancient China. According to legend, Emperor Shen Nung discovered the drink when leaves from a wild tree (1) into his pot of boiling water. He was pleasantly surprised by the refreshing aroma and flavor that (2) from the mixture. From these humble beginnings, tea eventually became a (3) of Chinese culture and a symbol of hospitality. It was not until the seventeenth century that tea was first (4) to Europe by Portuguese and Dutch traders. Initially, it was an expensive luxury that only the wealthy could (5) to buy. However, as trade routes expanded and production increased, tea became more accessible to the general public. In Britain, the custom of "afternoon tea" (6) root in the nineteenth century, largely thanks to the Duchess of Bedford. Today, tea is the most widely consumed beverage in the world, second only to water. Its popularity is (7) to both its varied flavors and its perceived health benefits. Whether enjoyed in a formal ceremony or as a quick morning pick-me-up, tea continues to (8) people together across different cultures and continents, maintaining its status as a truly global drink that has stood the test of time perfectly.

example.explanation: “Dates back” is the standard phrasal verb used to indicate when something originated.

questions.explanation: “Drifted” describes a light, slow movement through the air, perfect for a leaf.
“Resulted from” is the correct collocation to describe the outcome of a process.
“Cornerstone” is a figurative term for something fundamental or essential to a culture.
“Introduced to” is used when a new product or concept is brought to a place for the first time.
“Afford to buy” is the standard grammatical structure for having enough money.
“Take root” is an idiom meaning to become established or popular.
“Attributed to” means that something is seen as the cause of a situation.
“Bring people together” is a common collocation meaning to unite or collect individuals.
